title: Python timezone-aware datetime UTC handling
license_note: Fixture prose is purpose-written for connector tests and is not copied from StackOverflow posts. Replace with properly attributed public Stack Exchange API or StackPrinter captures for live runs.

questions.title: How should Python code compare aware datetimes and get current UTC safely?
questions.body_html: I have Python code that compares a database timestamp with the current UTC time. Sometimes it raises an offset-naive versus offset-aware datetime error. Should I use datetime.utcnow(), replace(tzinfo=...), or datetime.now(timezone.utc)?

comments.body_html: Question score and accepted status are useful signals, but they are not truth. Check newer edits and comments.

answers.body_html: Use timezone-aware datetime objects consistently. For current UTC, use datetime.now(timezone.utc). When converting an existing instant, use astimezone rather than pretending the timezone changed.

comments.body_html: Warning: replace(tzinfo=...) only labels a naive datetime; it does not convert the instant and can create an incorrect instant.

answers.body_html: Modern Python code should avoid datetime.utcnow because it returns a naive UTC datetime. Prefer datetime.now(timezone.utc), or datetime.now(UTC) on versions that expose UTC directly. This newer guidance supersedes older snippets that used utcnow for fresh code.

comments.body_html: For Python 3.12 and later, datetime.utcnow is deprecated in documentation guidance; carry the version context.

answers.body_html: A quick workaround is to strip tzinfo before comparing. This can silence the error but loses timezone meaning.

comments.body_html: Do not strip tzinfo as a general method; it can compare different instants as if they were the same local time.

linked_questions.title: Why does Python report offset-naive and offset-aware datetime comparison errors?
